title: The Everlasting Gospel
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The vision of Christ that thou dost see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is my visionן¿½s greatest enemy.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thine has a great hook nose like thine;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mine has a snub nose like to mine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thine is the Friend of all Mankind;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mine speaks in parables to the blind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thine loves the same world that mine hates;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy heaven doors are my hell gates.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Socrates taught what Meletus
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loathן¿½d as a nationן¿½s bitterest curse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Caiaphas was in his own mind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A benefactor to mankind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Both read the Bible day and night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thou readן¿½st black where I read white.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Jesus gentle, or did He
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give any marks of gentility?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When twelve years old He ran away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left His parents in dismay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When after three daysן¿½ sorrow found,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loud as Sinaiן¿½s trumpet-sound:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No earthly parents I confessן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My Heavenly Fatherן¿½s business!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ye understand not what I say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, angry, force Me to obey.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Obedience is a duty then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And favour gains with God and men.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John from the wilderness loud cried;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Satan gloried in his pride.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Come,ן¿½ said Satan, ן¿½come away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll soon see if youן¿½ll obey!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ John for disobedience bled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But you can turn the stones to bread.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s high king and Godן¿½s high priest
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall plant their glories in your breast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Caiaphas you will obey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If Herod you with bloody prey
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Feed with the sacrifice, and be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Obedient, fall down, worship me.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thunders and lightnings broke around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Jesusן¿½ voice in thundersן¿½ sound:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thus I seize the spiritual prey.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ye smiters with disease, make way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I come your King and God to seize,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is God a smiter with disease?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The God of this world ragן¿½d in vain:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He bound old Satan in His chain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, bursting forth, His furious ire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Became a chariot of fire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Throughout the land He took His course,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tracן¿½d diseases to their source.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He cursן¿½d the Scribe and Pharisee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trampling down hypocrisy.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereן¿½er His chariot took its way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There Gates of Death let in the Day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Broke down from every chain and bar;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Satan in His spiritual war
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Draggן¿½d at His chariot-wheels: loud howlן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The God of this world: louder rollן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The chariot-wheels, and louder still
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His voice was heard from Zionן¿½s Hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in His hand the scourge shone bright;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He scourgן¿½d the merchant Canaanite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From out the Temple of His Mind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in his body tight does bind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Satan and all his hellish crew;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus with wrath He did subdue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The serpent bulk of Natureן¿½s dross,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till He had nailן¿½d it to the Cross.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He took on sin in the Virginן¿½s womb
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And put it off on the Cross and tomb
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To be worshippן¿½d by the Church of Rome.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Jesus humble? or did He
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give any proofs of humility?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boast of high things with humble tone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And give with charity a stone?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When but a child He ran away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left His parents in dismay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they had wanderן¿½d three days long
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These were the words upon His tongue:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No earthly parents I confess:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am doing My Fatherן¿½s business.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the rich learnן¿½d Pharisee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came to consult Him secretly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon his heart with iron pen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He wrote ן¿½Ye must be born again.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was too proud to take a bribe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He spoke with authority, not like a Scribe.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He says with most consummate art
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Follow Me, I am meek and lowly of heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As that is the only way to escape
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The miserן¿½s net and the gluttonן¿½s trap.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What can be done with such desperate fools
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who follow after the heathen schools?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was standing by when Jesus died;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What I callן¿½d humility, they callן¿½d pride.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He who loves his enemies betrays his friends.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This surely is not what Jesus intends;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the sneaking pride of heroic schools,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Scribesן¿½ and Phariseesן¿½ virtuous rules;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For He acts with honest, triumphant pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And this is the cause that Jesus dies.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He did not die with Christian ease,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Asking pardon of His enemies:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If He had, Caiaphas would forgive;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sneaking submission can always live.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had only to say that God was the Devil,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Devil was God, like a Christian civil;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mild Christian regrets to the Devil confess
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For affronting him thrice in the wilderness;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had soon been bloody Caesarן¿½s elf,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And at last he would have been Caesar himself,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like Dr. Priestly and Bacon and Newtonן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor spiritual knowledge is not worth a button
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For thus the Gospel Sir Isaac confutes:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½God can only be known by His attributes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as for the indwelling of the Holy Ghost,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or of Christ and His Father, itן¿½s all a boast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pride, and vanity of the imagination,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That disdains to follow this worldן¿½s fashion.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To teach doubt and experiment
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Certainly was not what Christ meant.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was He doing all that time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From twelve years old to manly prime?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was He then idle, or the less
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ About His Fatherן¿½s business?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or was His wisdom held in scorn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before His wrath began to burn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In miracles throughout the land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That quite unnervן¿½d the Seraph band?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If He had been Antichrist, Creeping Jesus,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d have done anything to please us;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gone sneaking into synagogues,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And not usן¿½d the Elders and Priests like dogs;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But humble as a lamb or ass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Obeyן¿½d Himself to Caiaphas.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God wants not man to humble himself:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That is the trick of the Ancient Elf.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This is the race that Jesus ran:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humble to God, haughty to man,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cursing the Rulers before the people
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even to the Templeן¿½s highest steeple,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when He humbled Himself to God
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then descended the cruel rod.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If Thou Humblest Thyself, Thou humblest Me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou also dwellן¿½st in Eternity.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou art a Man: God is no more:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thy own Humanity learn to adore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For that is My spirit of life.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Awake, arise to spiritual strife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Thy revenge abroad display
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In terrors at the last Judgement Day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s mercy and long suffering
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is but the sinner to judgement to bring.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou on the Cross for them shalt prayן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And take revenge at the Last Day.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jesus replied, and thunders hurlן¿½d:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I never will pray for the world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Once I did so when I prayן¿½d in the Garden;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wishן¿½d to take with Me a bodily pardon.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can that which was of woman born,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the absence of the morn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the Soul fell into sleep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Archangels round it weep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shooting out against the light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fibres of a deadly night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Reasoning upon its own dark fiction,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In doubt which is self-contradiction?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humility is only doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And does the sun and moon blot out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rooting over with thorns and stems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The buried soul and all its gems.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This lifeן¿½s five windows of the soul
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Distorts the Heavens from pole to pole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And leads you to believe a lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When you see with, not throן¿½, the eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That was born in a night, to perish in a night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the soul slept in the beams of light.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did Jesus teach doubt? or did He
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give any lessons of philosophy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Charge Visionaries with deceiving,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or call men wise for not believing?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Jesus born of a Virgin pure
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With narrow soul and looks demure?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If He intended to take on sin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Mother should an harlot been,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just such a one as Magdalen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With seven devils in her pen.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or were Jew virgins still more cursן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And more sucking devils nursן¿½d?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or what was it which He took on
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That He might bring salvation?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A body subject to be tempted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From neither pain nor grief exempted;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or such a body as might not feel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The passions that with sinners deal?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, but they say He never fell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ask Caiaphas; for he can tell.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He mockן¿½d the Sabbath, and He mockן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Sabbathן¿½s God, and He unlockן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The evil spirits from their shrines,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And turnן¿½d fishermen to divines;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½erturnן¿½d the tent of secret sins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And its golden cords and pins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the bloody shrine of war
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pourן¿½d around from star to star,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Halls of justice, hating vice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the Devil combs his lice.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He turnן¿½d the devils into swine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That He might tempt the Jews to dine;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since which, a pig has got a look
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That for a Jew may be mistook.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Obey your parents.ן¿½ן¿½What says He?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Woman, what have I to do with thee?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No earthly parents I confess:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am doing my Fatherן¿½s business.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He scornן¿½d Earthן¿½s parents, scornן¿½d Earthן¿½s God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And mockן¿½d the one and the otherן¿½s rod;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His seventy Disciples sent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Against Religion and Governmentן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They by the sword of Justice fell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Him their cruel murderer tell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He left His fatherן¿½s trade to roam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wandן¿½ring vagrant without home;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thus He othersן¿½ labour stole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That He might live above control.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The publicans and harlots He
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Selected for His company,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And from the adulteress turnן¿½d away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s righteous law, that lost its prey.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Jesus chaste? or did He
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give any lessons of chastity?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Morning blushן¿½d fiery red:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mary was found in adulterous bed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Earth groanן¿½d beneath, and Heaven above
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trembled at discovery of Love.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jesus was sitting in Mosesן¿½ chair.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They brought the trembling woman there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Moses commands she be stonן¿½d to death.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was the sound of Jesusן¿½ breath?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He laid His hand on Mosesן¿½ law;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ancient Heavens, in silent awe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Writ with curses from pole to pole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All away began to roll.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Earth trembling and naked lay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In secret bed of mortal clay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On Sinai felt the Hand Divine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pulling back the bloody shrine;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she heard the breath of God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As she heard by Edenן¿½s flood:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Good and Evil are no more!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sinaiן¿½s trumpets cease to roar!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cease, finger of God, to write!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Heavens are not clean in Thy sight.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou art good, and Thou alone;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor may the sinner cast one stone.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To be good only, is to be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A God or else a Pharisee.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thou Angel of the Presence Divine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That didst create this Body of Mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherefore hast thou writ these laws
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And created Hellן¿½s dark jaws?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My Presence I will take from thee:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A cold leper thou shalt be.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ thou wast so pure and bright
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Heaven was impure in thy sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ thy oath turnן¿½d Heaven pale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ thy covenant built Hellן¿½s jail,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ thou didst all to chaos roll
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the Serpent for its soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still the breath Divine does move,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the breath Divine is Love.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mary, fear not! Let me see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The seven devils that torment thee.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hide not from My sight thy sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That forgiveness thou mayן¿½st win.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Has no man condemnן¿½d thee?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No man, Lord.ן¿½ ן¿½Then what is he
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who shall accuse thee? Come ye forth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fallen fiends of heavenly birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That have forgot your ancient love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And driven away my trembling Dove.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You shall bow before her feet;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You shall lick the dust for meat;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thoן¿½ you cannot love, but hate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shall be beggars at Loveן¿½s gate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was thy love? Let Me see it;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was it love or dark deceit?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Love too long from me has fled;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas dark deceit, to earn my bread;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas covet, or ן¿½twas custom, or
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some trifle not worth caring for;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That they may call a shame and sin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Loveן¿½s temple that God dwelleth in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hide in secret hidden shrine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The naked Human Form Divine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And render that a lawless thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On which the Soul expands its wing.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But this, O Lord, this was my sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When first I let these devils in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In dark pretence to chastity
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blaspheming Love, blaspheming Thee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thence rose secret adulteries,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thence did covet also rise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My sin Thou hast forgiven me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Canst Thou forgive my blasphemy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Canst Thou return to this dark hell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in my burning bosom dwell?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And canst Thou die that I may live?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And canst Thou pity and forgive?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then rollן¿½d the shadowy Man away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the limbs of Jesus, to make them His prey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An ever devouring appetite,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Glittering with festering venoms bright;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crying ן¿½Crucify this cause of distress,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who donן¿½t keep the secrets of holiness!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mental powers by diseases we bind;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But He heals the deaf, the dumb, and the blind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whom God has afflicted for secret ends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He comforts and heals and calls them friends.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, when Jesus was crucified,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then was perfected His galling pride.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In three nights He devourן¿½d His prey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And still He devours the body of clay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For dust and clay is the Serpentן¿½s meat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which never was made for Man to eat.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seeing this False Christ, in fury and passion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I made my voice heard all over the nation.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What are thoseן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am sure this Jesus will not do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Either for Englishman or Jew.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Blake represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Blake's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
